title: Why Professional Development Matters for Museum Professionals
content:
Learn how investing in your growth can transform your career — and your institution.

Museums are at a crossroads. Visitor expectations are changing, digital transformation is reshaping how collections are shared, and conversations around inclusion, decolonisation, and community engagement are redefining what museums are for. In this environment, professional development isn’t a nice-to-have for museum professionals — it’s essential.

Whether you’re a curator, educator, collections manager, digital lead, or in museum leadership, continuing to learn is one of the most powerful things you can do for your career, your team, and the communities you serve.

This article explores what professional development means for people working in museums, why it matters, and how to make the most of the opportunities available to you.

creator: Manual Charr

title: When the Museum Comes to Prison: Oxford’s ‘In the Frame’ Programme
content:
How the University of Oxford’s Gardens, Libraries and Museums is using handling objects and creative activities to support wellbeing inside HMP Bullingdon and HMP Grendon.

What happens when a museum removes its walls entirely — and brings its objects into one of the most challenging environments imaginable? For the community engagement team at the University of Oxford Gardens, Libraries and Museums (GLAM), the answer has been a programme called In the Frame, and the results have been quietly remarkable.

From Pilot to Programme

GLAM’s work in the criminal justice sector didn’t begin with a polished blueprint. Since 2022, the team had been running occasional sessions in Bullingdon Prison, bringing handling objects in to stimulate conversation. A social prescribing pilot followed, run in partnership with NHS England Health and Justice — but as community engagement officer Sarah Levete acknowledges, that pilot didn

creator: Manual Charr

title: How the Art Institute of Chicago Is Building Mindfulness Into the Museum Experience
content:
What happens when you design for “how do I be with the art?” rather than “what is the art?” — and why a live gallery programme and a digital audio tour are better together than either could be alone.

There’s a question that museum professionals have been circling for years: how do you make a visit feel personally meaningful, rather than simply educational? For Marielle Epstein, Assistant Director of Interpretation at the Art Institute of Chicago, the answer came from an unexpected direction — mindfulness.

Want more insights like this? Explore our upcoming virtual conferences here

 A Different Kind of Question

The Art Institute welcomes nearly 1.5 million visitors each year. Its collection spans centuries and cultures, and its programmes are wide-ranging. But Epstein and her colleague Gina Giambalvo, the museum’s Digital Studio Director, f
snippet: What happens when you design for “how do I be with the art?” rather than “what is the art?” — and why a live gallery programme and a digital audio tour are better together than either could be alone.
creator: Manual Charr

title: Art, Empathy, and Healthcare Worker Wellbeing at the Whitney Museum
content:
The Whitney Museum of American Art has built two distinctive partnerships with New York hospital systems — and the results are transforming how medical professionals relate to patients, colleagues, and themselves.

There’s a question that rarely gets asked in discussions about healthcare: who looks after the people doing the looking after? Since the pandemic, awareness of mental health provision for patients has grown significantly. But the wellbeing of the healthcare workers themselves — the nurses, surgeons, researchers, administrators, and students holding the system together — has often been an afterthought. The Whitney Museum of American Art in New York is trying to change that.

Two Partnerships, One Mission

Through two distinct programmes, the Whitney has built meaningful collaborations with major hospital organisations in New York City. The first is with NYC Health + Hospitals, the largest hospital network in New York, which pro
snippet: The Whitney Museum of American Art has built two distinctive partnerships with New York hospital systems — and the results are transforming how medical professionals relate to patients, colleagues, an
creator: Manual Charr

title: When the Museum Becomes a Group: Stapferhaus’s Experiment in Belonging
content:
How a Swiss museum partnered with a healthcare clinic to create a space where strangers could explore what it means to connect.

There’s a question that sits at the heart of contemporary public life: in a world that feels increasingly fragmented, how do we move from “me” to “we”? For the Stapferhaus in Lenzburg, Switzerland, that question became not just a curatorial theme but the basis for an unusually intimate experiment — one that asked thirty strangers to sit in a circle, without a script or a plan, and see what happened.

An Unlikely Collaboration

The Stapferhaus describes itself as a place for encounter and dialogue. Its current exhibition, Health: An Exhibition with Side Effects, invites visitors to explore the many dimensions of wellbeing — what health means, who defines it, and how societies might build systems that serve everyone. To develop the exhibition, the team spoke widely:

creator: Manual Charr

title: National Gallery Singapore, Youth People and Mental Health
content:
What if an art museum’s role extended beyond curating masterpieces and educating school groups? What if its platforms — its walls, its festivals, its expertise — could be turned over to young people who are rarely seen, let alone heard? That’s the question at the heart of a collaboration between National Gallery Singapore and social service agency Fei Yue Community Services, presented at a recent MuseumNext conference by Mian Tze Kng and Zoe Tee.

From School Programmes to the Margins

National Gallery Singapore has, since 2021, been developing Strength Through Art (stART), a programme designed to improve the visual and emotional literacy of young people. In its original form, stART was delivered to youth in mainstream schools, following a three-phase framework: Name, Frame, and Reframe. Participants were guided to identify and articulate their emotions, connect those feelings to artworks in th

creator: Manual Charr

title: Why We Need Museums Now More Than Ever — The Importance of Museums
content:
In today’s uncertain times, museums can act as an anchor in the storm.

To those who aren’t as passionate about the power of museums as readers of this blog, it can often seem that such institutions are merely places where forgotten objects go to enjoy their final years.

But despite this, there’s a solid case to be made that the museum is more relevant today than ever. From addressing critical social issues to transforming how we see the future, the humble museum has the power to reflect and shape our society. Here are five reasons why we need museums now more than ever.

Museums Help Us to Learn From the Past

First and foremost, museums and galleries provide an insight into humankind’s history. While no museum can claim to provide a complete picture, the lessons we can learn from past events, wonders, and tragedies are priceless.

creator: Rebecca Carlsson

title: Carbon Reduction Strategies for Museums
content:
As museums confront the realities of the climate crisis, the conversation around carbon reduction has shifted from aspiration to action. Institutions are increasingly recognising that environmental responsibility is inseparable from their public mission.

The Green Museums Summit brought this shift into focus through detailed examples from museums already embedding sustainability into daily operations. Their experiences offer a practical and inspirational roadmap for peers across the sector.

The Imperial War Museums (IWM) and Miraikan, the National Museum of Emerging Science and Innovation in Tokyo, provided two of the Summit’s most comprehensive case studies. Despite operating in very different contexts, both organisations share the conviction

creator: Manual Charr

title: What a Dementia Programme Can Teach Us About the Hidden Power of Museums
content:
House of Memories Tour / Image: Pete Carr / Courtesy of National Museums Liverpool

What do railways, forests, Eurovision, and old suitcases have in common? On the surface, not much. But for people living with dementia in Liverpool—and increasingly around the world—these seemingly ordinary touchpoints are proving to be extraordinary tools for connection, memory, and care.

This is the insight driving House of Memories, a dementia awareness programme from National Museums Liverpool. Since 2012, the initiative has turned museums into something more than repositories of the past: they have become engines of empathy, bridges between health and culture, and laborat

creator: Manual Charr

title: Ten Creative Programme Ideas That Museums Can Offer Adults
content:
Why should kids have all the fun? Museums have an incredible potential to not only educate adults, but also engage and entertain them. Through using collection objects and community spaces, museums can draw in adult audiences to learn, play, create, unwind and more…

In this article, we’ll share ten creative programmes ideas to engage your adult visitors. From multilingual classes to virtual date nights, we hope you’ll glean some inspiration to create your own dynamic programmes.

Innovative Lecture Series in London’s Museums

London’s Victoria & Albert Museum lecture series takes inspiration from the collection and current exhibitions. They offer both physical and digital talks with varying price points to maximise accessibility. These lectures are designed for curious minds that want to process museum themes and work through ideas post-visit or exhibition.
snippet: Why should kids have all the fun? Museums have an incredible potential to not only educate adults, but also engage and entertain them. Through using collection objects and community spaces, museums ca
creator: Devon Rose Turner

title: 20 Essential Books for Museum Studies Students
content:
For students pursuing a career in museums, building a strong foundation of knowledge is crucial. From curation and exhibition design to education, activism, and ethics, understanding the complexities of museum work will prepare you for a dynamic and impactful career.

This list of 20 essential books is designed specifically for museum studies students—offering insights, practical advice, and inspiration to help you excel in your studies and future career.

1. Museums as Agents of Change

By Mike Murawski
